title: Integrality Properties of Topological Fundamental Groups
speaker: Hélène Esnault
abstract: The talk is based on joint work with Michael Groechenig and Johan de Jong. We single out integrality properties of the topological fundamental group of smooth quasi-projective complex varieties which rely on the Langlands program (both arithmetic and geometric) and yield an obstruction for a finitely presented group to be the fundamental group of a smooth quasiprojective variety.
tags: Conference on 100 Years of Noetherian Rings

proof_stages.label: Geometric finitely presented groups
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines finitely presented groups, observes that pi_1 of a smooth connected quasi-projective complex variety is finitely presented, and formulates the problem of finding obstructions to such groups being geometric.

proof_stages.label: Weak integrality as a group obstruction
proof_stages.summary: A finitely presented group is defined to be weakly integral if the existence of an irreducible complex representation of given rank and finite determinant order forces integral ell-adic representations with the same discrete data for every prime ell; geometric groups satisfy this property, and failures give non-geometric groups.

proof_stages.label: Quasi-projective local-system theorem
proof_stages.summary: The central geometric theorem is stated: for a smooth quasi-projective complex variety, an irreducible complex local system with finite determinant and quasi-unipotent monodromy at infinity gives, for every prime ell, an irreducible integral ell-adic local system with the corresponding determinant and boundary data.

proof_stages.label: Betti moduli and reduction to finite characteristic
proof_stages.summary: The proof passes to the Betti moduli space of local systems with fixed rank, determinant order, and boundary monodromy, spreads it over a number ring, and chooses suitable reductions whose tame fundamental groups are related to the original topological fundamental group by specialization.

proof_stages.label: Mazur deformation and de Jong arithmetic lift
proof_stages.summary: At an absolutely irreducible residual point, the formal completion of the moduli space is identified with Mazur's deformation space, and de Jong's theorem/conjectural mechanism produces an irreducible arithmetic local system over a finite field with the prescribed deformation conditions.

proof_stages.label: Deligne companions
proof_stages.summary: Using Deligne's companion philosophy, proved via the Langlands program for curves and Drinfeld's extension to smooth higher-dimensional varieties, the arithmetic local system is transported to companions for any desired auxiliary prime while preserving determinant, local monodromy, and irreducibility.

proof_stages.label: Return to topology and integral lattices
proof_stages.summary: The companion is pulled back along the specialization/profinite comparison to the original topological fundamental group, and compactness of the ell-adic image gives a stable lattice, yielding a representation over the integral closure of Z_ell and completing the proof.

proof_stages.label: Weakly arithmetic local systems and density
proof_stages.summary: The proof suggests a notion of weakly arithmetic complex local systems, and the speaker concludes that these form a Zariski-dense set of points in the relevant Betti moduli spaces, giving a possible intermediate arithmetic substitute for genuinely arithmetic local systems.

examples.name: Punctured projective line realizing free groups
examples.note: P^1 minus finitely many points is used to make concrete that familiar finitely presented groups can occur as fundamental groups of smooth quasi-projective varieties.
examples.engagement_reason: She states that a free group can be obtained from a projective line with finitely many points removed, but does not compute or verify the fundamental group.

passes.reason: A punctured projective line is a standard genus-zero open curve in an infinite family, used positively to realize free groups geometrically.

examples.name: Finitely generated abelian quotients via G_m
examples.note: The decomposition of a finitely generated abelian quotient as Z^b plus torsion is illustrated by the fact that Z is pi_1 of G_m(C), with the projective case contrasted by the evenness constraint from Hodge theory.
examples.engagement_reason: She decomposes the finitely generated abelian quotient into a free part and torsion and explains its geometric realization using pi_1(\mathbb G_m)=\mathbb Z, with the projective even-rank obstruction noted.

passes.reason: The use of G_m/algebraic tori gives a standard infinite-family realization of finitely generated abelian fundamental groups, not a refutation.

examples.name: Displayed two-generator one-relator group pi_0
examples.note: The one-relator group pi_0 is used as an application of weak integrality: its two SL_2 representations are not 2-adically integral, so the group is not geometric.
examples.engagement_reason: She applies the weak-integrality obstruction to the specific two-generator one-relator group, discussing its rank-two irreducible representations and their failure of 2-adic integrality.

passes.reason: The two-generator one-relator group is structurally not a sporadic exception, but is deployed as a non-geometric group obstructed by weak integrality.

examples.name: Deligne-Toledo non-residually finite examples
examples.note: Examples with nontrivial kernel in pi to its profinite completion illustrate that representation-theoretic obstructions necessarily miss some information about finitely presented groups.
examples.engagement_reason: Deligne and Toledo's non-residually finite examples are cited only to show that the profinite-completion kernel can be nontrivial.

passes.reason: The Deligne-Toledo examples are used to show residual finiteness/profinite detection can fail, while not being classification-exceptional objects.

examples.name: Normal surface singularity with exceptional cycle of P^1s
examples.note: In response to a question about singular varieties, this surface singularity shows why companions for normal nonsmooth varieties are hard: Frobenius characteristic polynomials do not see the extra discrete monodromy around the exceptional cycle.
examples.engagement_reason: In the question period she analyzes the normal surface singularity with a cycle of \mathbb P^1s, explaining trivial restrictions on components versus nontrivial cycle monodromy not detected by Frobenius polynomials.

passes.reason: The normal surface singularity with a cycle of rational exceptional curves is a singular/degenerate configuration used as an obstruction to naive companion constructions on normal varieties.

The normal surface singularity collapses an exceptional cycle of rational curves and is used as an obstruction showing monodromy in the singular case is not detected by the usual local/Frobenius data.
The normal surface singularity collapses a cycle of rational curves in its resolution and is used as an obstruction where monodromy is not detected by Frobenius data.
The normal surface singularity with a cycle of rational curves is a standard type used as an obstruction/enemy case for extending companion constructions to singular varieties.
the normal surface singularity comes from a collapsed cycle of rational exceptional curves and is used as an enemy case where companion data miss monodromy
The normal surface singularity with a cycle of P^1s is used as an obstruction to extending companion constructions to singular normal varieties, not as a sporadic exception.
The normal surface singularity is used as an obstruction to extending companions, and its resolution collapses a rational cycle of P^1s.
